courses.title: Mechanical Vibrations
courses.summary: The theory under the analyzer screen: free and forced vibration, damping, multi-degree systems, rotor basics, and isolation — taught so Year 4's condition-monitoring course lands on solid mathematics.

lessons.t: The single degree of freedom system
lessons.scope: Equation of motion from Newton and from energy. Natural frequency's dependence on stiffness and mass — and on nothing else. The model that explains half of all machine vibration behavior.
Viscous damping and the three response regimes. Logarithmic decrement measured from a bump test. Damping ratio as the machine's shock absorber rating.
The frequency response function derived: amplitude and phase versus forcing frequency. Resonance quantified by Q. Why passing through resonance on run-up is survivable but dwelling is not.
Unbalance forcing derived from eccentric mass; response above and below critical speed. The phase flip through resonance. First contact with rotor dynamics language.
Base-excited systems and force transmissibility derived. The isolation region above √2 times natural frequency. Why soft mounts protect and when they betray.
Isolator selection: static deflection, natural frequency, damping trade-offs. Inertia blocks and their sizing logic. A compressor installation isolated on paper.

lessons.t: Two degrees of freedom and mode shapes
lessons.scope: Coupled equations, natural frequencies, and mode shapes solved via the eigenproblem promised in Math II. Beating between close modes. The concept structure for all modal analysis.

lessons.t: Multi-DOF and continuous systems in outline
lessons.scope: Modal superposition as the organizing idea. Beam and shaft natural frequencies from standard results. Why long rotors have families of criticals.
Shaft-train torsional models; natural frequencies of two- and three-inertia systems. Excitation from VFDs and reciprocating machines. The invisible vibration that breaks couplings.
Accelerometer, velocity, and displacement measurement physics; integration between them. Mounting effects on usable frequency range. The hardware bridge to Year 4's analysis course.
Resonance, beats, looseness nonlinearity, and self-excited whirl shown as measured signatures. Each tied back to its lesson's mathematics. The course's theory validated against plant reality.

courses.title: Instrumentation & Process Control
courses.summary: The control loop as plumbing and paperwork: P&ID literacy, transmitters and control valves, loop tuning in the field, and the safety instrumented systems that sit above regulation — EQUATE-style process-plant territory.

lessons.t: The process control loop end to end
lessons.scope: Sensor, transmitter, controller, and final element as one signal chain. Loop drawings versus P&IDs. Every element's failure mode inventoried.

ISA-style symbols and tag grammar decoded — the identification letters, loop numbers, and line conventions that make a P&ID legible. Equipment, instruments, and interlocks traced through a real sheet. The drawing a process plant hands you on day one, read without flinching.

Globe, ball, and butterfly bodies; inherent versus installed characteristic derived from the pressure-ratio argument. Cv sizing in outline; cavitation and flashing in valves. Positioners and their diagnostics.
Ranging, elevation/suppression, and impulse-line practice consolidated. Smart transmitter diagnostics used properly. Calibration versus validation on live plants.
Process identification from bump tests: gain, time constant, dead time. Lambda and reaction-curve tuning applied to level, flow, and temperature archetypes. Dead time as the real enemy, named.
Cascade, ratio, and feedforward — each derived from a disturbance argument. Split-range and override control where hardware demands it. Reading these structures off a P&ID.
DCS versus PLC/SCADA delineated honestly. Controllers, I/O, and HMI stations in a process-plant architecture. Alarm management at plant scale — the rationalization discipline.
The protection layers above control; SIS separated from BPCS by design. SIL concepts and proof testing in outline. Trip logic read from cause-and-effect diagrams.
pH, conductivity, and gas analyzers in outline; sample systems as the real failure point. Analyzer maintenance economics. Where analyzers gate product release.
Calibration programs, drift trending, and smart diagnostics as condition monitoring for instruments. Instrument criticality ranking. The instrument technician–reliability engineer interface.

For one control loop: P&ID extract, instrument datasheets, valve sizing sketch, tuning plan, and test procedure. The document set of a small management-of-change. Instrumentation practiced as engineering, not adjustment.

courses.title: Industrial Robotics
courses.summary: Robots as production equipment: kinematics enough to reason, plus the practical layer — end effectors, safety systems, palletizing and welding cells, and what a robot cell demands from maintenance.

lessons.scope: Articulated, SCARA, delta, and cartesian architectures with their sweet spots. Axes, envelopes, and payload ratings read from datasheets. Repeatability versus accuracy — the specification that matters.
Frames and transformations; forward kinematics in concept. The inverse problem and singularities — why some poses are unreachable at speed. Enough mathematics to respect the controller.
Servo axes, gearing, and absolute encoders; mastering and calibration procedures. Backlash and its symptoms in path quality. The maintenance anatomy of an arm.
Grippers — pneumatic, servo, vacuum, magnetic — matched to product. Tool changers and dress packs, the wear items nobody budgets. Payload and center-of-gravity arithmetic.
Jogging, frames, and point teaching; motion types and speed/approximation trade-offs. Program structure for cells with variants. Offline programming in outline.
Guarding, light curtains, and safety-rated monitored stops; collaborative modes with their real limits. Risk assessment for a cell. Safety as the cell's first design input.
End-of-line palletizing patterns and cycle-time budgets. Conveyor tracking basics. The robot cell most likely to appear at the end of a KDD-style packaging line.
Torch packages, seam paths, and weave parameters; positioners as the second robot. Consumable and TCP maintenance. Robotic arc welding on structural work of the Kirby kind.
The robot inside the PLC's state machine: handshakes, permissives, and fault recovery paths. Peripheral devices and fixturing. Cycle-time analysis against the line's takt.
Preventive tasks: lubrication, belts, batteries, dress-pack inspection. Error logs and drift trends as early warnings. What a robot's reliability program looks like on paper.

courses.title: Reliability Engineering I
courses.summary: Quantified failure: reliability mathematics, Weibull analysis of real data, system modeling, and FMEA/FMECA — the analytical core of the reliability engineer role this pathway targets.

lessons.scope: Reliability, CDF, PDF, and hazard rate defined and interrelated with derivations. The bathtub curve as hazard-rate biography. Reading each function's story from the others.
Constant hazard rate and its memoryless consequence proven. MTBF's honest meaning under the exponential. Where the model applies — and the electronics-versus-wearout boundary.

The Weibull distribution with shape and scale parameters read as physics: β<1 infant mortality, β≈1 random, β>1 wearout. Median-rank plotting of a real failure dataset, censored points handled correctly. The analysis that turns a failure log into a maintenance strategy argument.
Data collection sins: mixed failure modes, missing suspensions, bad timestamps. Goodness of fit with judgment. Confidence bounds that keep conclusions honest.

Series, parallel, and k-out-of-n structures derived from probability first principles; reliability block diagrams of real equipment trains. Redundancy's diminishing returns and common-cause caveat. A pumping station modeled end to end as the condensed example.
Repairable-system measures; availability from failure and repair distributions. Maintainability as design property — access, modularity, error-proofing. Where MTTR is really spent, measured.

Failure modes and effects analysis built properly: functions, functional failures, modes, effects, severity–occurrence–detection scoring, and the RPN's known abuses. Criticality analysis as the FMECA extension. One equipment FMEA worked as the condensed example — the template interviews ask you to narrate.
Top events, gates, and minimal cut sets; quantification with basic-event probabilities. FTA versus FMEA — deductive versus inductive. Protective-system failures as the classic application.

Reliability-centered maintenance's seven questions and its decision logic: condition-based first, time-based where age matters, run-to-failure where consequences permit, redesign where nothing works. Hidden failures and failure-finding intervals. The framework that disciplines every PM program worth the name.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Reliability Engineering; SMRP CMRP framework

lessons.scope: Life testing and accelerated testing in outline; reliability growth tracking during commissioning. Burn-in's logic and limits. Evidence standards for 'the fix worked'.
Maintenance-induced failures: the uncomfortable data. Error-provocative designs and procedures. Designing tasks so the night shift succeeds.

From a provided failure log: clean, classify, Weibull-fit, model the system, and recommend strategy changes with quantified backing. The full Reliability I toolkit exercised once. Deliverable formatted as a plant engineering report.

courses.title: Corrosion & Degradation
courses.summary: The Gulf's tax on steel: electrochemical fundamentals, the corrosion forms bestiary, marine and coastal service, and the defenses — materials, coatings, cathodic protection, inhibitors — that asset integrity budgets buy.
taught_from: Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 17; NPTEL: Corrosion-related materials courses (nptel.ac.in)

lessons.scope: Anodes, cathodes, and the corrosion cell derived from half-reactions. Galvanic series versus EMF series — the practical one wins. Corrosion rate units and conversions.

Chlorides, heat, humidity, and marine atmosphere as the region's standing corrosion load. Pitting and crevice attack on stainless; CUI — corrosion under insulation — as the hidden budget-eater; seawater cooling circuits. The condensed example: ranking degradation threats for a coastal plant's asset register.

The 'benign' uniform mode and when it isn't; galvanic couples with area-ratio arithmetic; crevices as chemistry chambers. Design details that create or kill crevices. Gasket and fastener implications.
Pit initiation on passive films; chloride's special villainy. PREN as a stainless selection number. Why pits sink ships that thickness surveys called healthy.

Stress corrosion cracking's three-ingredient recipe; chloride SCC of austenitics; hydrogen embrittlement and sulfide stress cracking in outline. Why EAC failures are sudden and forensic. Materials–environment pairings memorized by the industry.
Erosion-corrosion, cavitation damage, and fretting. Velocity limits in piping specs explained. The pump-impeller damage gallery interpreted.
Oxidation kinetics and scale protectiveness; sulfidation and fuel-ash attack in outline. Creep–corrosion interactions at furnace conditions. The melt-shop and fired-heater materials problem.
Surface preparation standards as the real quality variable; coating families and their service envelopes. Inspection: holiday testing and DFT. Coating failure modes read like weld defects.
lessons.src: Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 17; NPTEL corrosion content

lessons.scope: Sacrificial and impressed-current CP with the electrochemistry made explicit. CP surveys in outline. Inhibitors and chemical treatment where coatings can't go.

Coupons, ER probes, and thickness-monitoring locations; corrosion rates fed into remaining-life arithmetic. Corrosion management systems as documents that direct money. The bridge into Year 4's asset integrity course.

courses.title: Production Planning & Control
courses.summary: The factory's calendar: forecasting, capacity, scheduling, inventory, and MRP — how production commitments are made and kept, and where maintenance windows must be fought for inside them.
taught_from: NPTEL: Production and Operations Management-family courses (nptel.ac.in)

lessons.scope: Job shop, batch, line, and continuous production with their planning personalities. Layout types and material flow. Where each Kuwait anchor plant sits on this spectrum.
Moving averages and exponential smoothing with error tracking. Seasonality in outline. Forecasts as inputs to be doubted, not truths.
Design versus effective capacity; utilization and efficiency separated. Takt time computed and confronted with cycle times. Bottleneck identification as the first act of scheduling.
Level versus chase strategies costed. The master production schedule as the plant's promise ledger. Frozen zones and why they exist.
EOQ derived and then handled with care; safety stock from service levels; ABC classification. Holding-cost honesty. The same mathematics that will price spare parts in Year 4.
Bills of materials, lead-time offsetting, and the MRP explosion worked by hand once. ERP as MRP grown up. Data accuracy as the system's oxygen.
Dispatching rules and their trade-offs; Gantt logic and finite capacity. Sequence-dependent setups — the changeover matrix. Why the schedule is always wrong and still necessary.
Precedence diagrams, station assignment, and balance efficiency. Rebalancing for demand changes. The packaging-line application: staffing a KDD-style line to takt.

Planned windows, opportunistic maintenance, and shutdown coordination as scheduling problems. The production–maintenance negotiation formalized. Availability commitments read from both sides of the table.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Production and Operations Management; SMRP framework

lessons.scope: Schedule adherence, throughput, WIP, and lead time as a coherent measure set. Little's law derived and used. The metrics OEE will join in Year 4.
A multi-product line planned through one quarter: forecast, MPS, MRP run, schedule, and a maintenance-window negotiation. Deliverables in planner's formats. The course's tools used as one system.
